input.new_string:
def test_markup_identity_with_grammar_is_suppressed(self, tmp_path):
        """A repo whose identity is a markup/DSL parser is not a whole-language
        reference even with a grammar file (gate L)."""
        pkg = write_package_json(tmp_path, {
            "name": "graphql",
            "main": "index.js",
        })
        result = mod.detect(REPO_URL, [pkg], ["src/schema.g4"], [])
        assert result["shape"] != "language-reference"


# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# language-reference — TREE-TRIAD rung (Rung B, issue #427)
#
# Hand-written compilers (rustc, TypeScript, Go) declare no parser-generator
# dependency and ship no grammar file. They are caught by a dedicated compiler/
# directory holding a lexer+parser+AST triad plus a codegen/VM/type-checker
# member. The six false-positive controls (webpack, postcss, prettier,
# graphql-js, dart-sass, marked) each have a near-miss shape and must stay out.
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Faithful reconstructions of each repo's real compiler tree.
RUST_TREE = [
    "compiler/", "compiler/rustc_lexer/", "compiler/rustc_parse/",
    "compiler/rustc_ast/", "compiler/rustc_codegen_llvm/",
]
TS_TREE = [
    "src/compiler/", "src/compiler/scanner.ts", "src/compiler/parser.ts",
    "src/compiler/binder.ts", "src/compiler/checker.ts",
]
GO_TREE = [
    "cmd/compile/", "cmd/compile/internal/syntax/",
    "cmd/compile/internal/syntax/scanner.go",
    "cmd/compile/internal/syntax/parser.go",
    "go/ast/", "go/ast/ast.go", "go/types/", "go/types/check.go",
]


class TestLanguageReferenceTreeTriadRung:
    def test_rust_compiler_triad(self):
        result = mod.detect(REPO_URL, [], [], RUST_TREE)
        assert_result_shape(result)
        assert result["shape"] == "language-reference"
        assert any(s.startswith("tree_triad:") for s in result["signals"])

    def test_typescript_triad_outranks_bin(self, tmp_path):
        """TypeScript ships `tsc` (a bin) — the triad must outrank reference-app."""
        pkg = write_package_json(tmp_path, {
            "name": "typescript",
            "bin": {"tsc": "./bin/tsc.js", "tsserver": "./bin/tsserver.js"},
        })
        result = mod.detect(REPO_URL, [pkg], [], TS_TREE)
        assert result["shape"] == "language-reference"

    def test_go_toolchain_triad(self):
        result = mod.detect(REPO_URL, [], [], GO_TREE)
        assert result["shape"] == "language-reference"

    # --- negative controls: each near-miss must stay out ---

    def test_webpack_parser_file_not_dir(self, tmp_path):
        """lib/Parser.js is a FILE, not a Parser/ dir; plus acorn dep (gate G)."""
        pkg = write_package_json(tmp_path, {
            "name": "webpack",
            "bin": {"webpack": "./bin.js"},
            "dependencies": {"acorn": "8.0.0"},
        })
        result = mod.detect(REPO_URL, [pkg], [], ["lib/", "lib/Parser.js"])
        assert result["shape"] != "language-reference"

    def test_postcss_no_compiler_dir(self, tmp_path):
        pkg = write_package_json(tmp_path, {"name": "postcss", "main": "lib/postcss.js"})
        tree = ["lib/", "lib/tokenize.js", "lib/parser.js", "lib/node.js"]
        result = mod.detect(REPO_URL, [pkg], [], tree)
        assert result["shape"] != "language-reference"

    def test_graphql_js_src_language_not_compiler(self, tmp_path):
        pkg = write_package_json(tmp_path, {"name": "graphql", "main": "index.js"})
        tree = ["src/language/", "src/language/lexer.ts",
                "src/language/parser.ts", "src/language/ast.ts"]
        result = mod.detect(REPO_URL, [pkg], [], tree)
        assert result["shape"] != "language-reference"

    def test_dart_sass_compiler_is_a_file(self, tmp_path):
        pkg = write_package_json(tmp_path, {"name": "sass", "main": "sass.dart.js"})
        tree = ["lib/src/", "lib/src/parse/", "lib/src/compiler.dart",
                "lib/src/syntax.dart"]
        result = mod.detect(REPO_URL, [pkg], [], tree)
        assert result["shape"] != "language-reference"

    def test_marked_bare_src_dir(self, tmp_path):
        pkg = write_package_json(tmp_path, {"name": "marked", "main": "lib/marked.js"})
        tree = ["src/", "src/Lexer.ts", "src/Parser.ts", "src/Tokenizer.ts"]
        result = mod.detect(REPO_URL, [pkg], [], tree)
        assert result["shape"] != "language-reference"

    def test_w_gate_is_load_bearing(self, tmp_path):
        """A repo with a real compiler/ dir AND a full lexer+parser+AST triad but
        NO codegen/VM/type-checker (a markdown-shaped lib) must stay out. Proves
        gate W, not the compiler-dir alone, is the discriminator."""
        pkg = write_package_json(tmp_path, {"name": "quaxmark", "main": "index.js"})
        tree = ["src/compiler/", "src/compiler/lexer.ts",
                "src/compiler/parser.ts", "src/compiler/ast.ts"]
        result = mod.detect(REPO_URL, [pkg], [], tree)
        assert result["shape"] != "language-reference"

Whole-language toolchains like rustc, TypeScript, and the Go compiler
declare no parser-generator dependency and ship no grammar file, so
neither the dependency rung nor the grammar rung reaches them. Add a
tree-triad rung that fires language-reference from directory/structural
signals, ranked first so it outranks the bin->reference-app rung (a
language ships a compiler binary).

The rung requires three structural gates together, so an ordinary
library that merely contains a src/parser/ directory cannot trip it:
- a DEDICATED compiler directory (compiler/, src/compiler/, cmd/compile/,
  internal/syntax/, or a case-sensitive Parser/) matched as a real
  directory by path-tail — a Parser.js or compiler.dart FILE never counts;
- a lexer+parser+AST triad with the parser mandatory;
- a corroborating codegen / VM / type-checker member, so a markdown or
  CSS parser (lexer+parser+AST only) is excluded.

Combined with the delegating-consumer and markup-identity gates, the six
near-miss controls (webpack, postcss, prettier, graphql-js, dart-sass,
marked) each stay out via at least two independent gates, while
rust-lang/rust, microsoft/TypeScript, and golang/go classify on their
intentional compiler structure rather than an incidental dependency.

Refs #427

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git log --oneline -1

def parse_go_mod(text: str) -> tuple[list[Dep], list[str]]:
    """Extract `require (...)` and single-line `require` entries from go.mod."""
    warnings: list[str] = []
    deps: list[Dep] = []
    # Single-line: `require <module> <version>`
    for line in text.splitlines():
        m = re.match(
            r"^\s*require\s+(?P<name>[A-Za-z0-9_./\-]+)\s+(?P<version>v[\w.\-+]+|[\w.\-+]+)",
            line,
        )
        if m:
            deps.append({"name": m.group("name"), "version": m.group("version")})

    # Block: `require ( ... )`
    for block in re.finditer(r"require\s*\(\s*(.*?)\s*\)", text, re.DOTALL):
        body = block.group(1)
        for line in body.splitlines():
            stripped = line.strip()
            if not stripped or stripped.startswith("//"):
                continue
            # exclude "// indirect" suffix from version
            m = _GO_REQUIRE_LINE.match(stripped)
            if m:
                deps.append({"name": m.group("name"), "version": m.group("version")})
    return deps, warnings


def parse_pom_xml(text: str) -> tuple[list[Dep], list[str]]:
    """Best-effort regex extraction of <dependency>...</dependency> blocks."""
    warnings: list[str] = []
    deps: list[Dep] = []
    for block in re.finditer(r"<dependency>(.*?)</dependency>", text, re.DOTALL):
        body = block.group(1)
        scope_m = re.search(r"<scope>\s*(.*?)\s*</scope>", body)
        # skip test/provided/system scopes — runtime + compile + (no-scope) are production
        if scope_m and scope_m.group(1).strip().lower() in {"test", "provided", "system"}:
            continue
        gid = re.search(r"<groupId>\s*(.*?)\s*</groupId>", body)
        aid = re.search(r"<artifactId>\s*(.*?)\s*</artifactId>", body)
        ver = re.search(r"<version>\s*(.*?)\s*</version>", body)
        if not gid or not aid:
            continue
        name = f"{gid.group(1).strip()}:{aid.group(1).strip()}"
        version = ver.group(1).strip() if ver else None
        deps.append({"name": name, "version": version})
    return deps, warnings


_GRADLE_DEP = re.compile(
    r"""(?P<scope>implementation|api|compile|runtimeOnly)
        \s*\(?\s*
        (?:['"])
        (?P<coord>[^'"]+)
        (?:['"])
        """,

This filtered, comma-joined list of resolved paths is fed to shape detection in §3. 1	# Shape Detection Reference
2	
3	Reference document for invoking `skf-shape-detect.py` — the shared shape classification module. Loaded by `step-auto-scope.md` for auto-scope analysis.
4	
5	## Invocation Contract
6	
7	**Script:** `src/shared/scripts/skf-shape-detect.py`
8	
9	**Command:**
10	```
11	uv run src/shared/scripts/skf-shape-detect.py --repo-url <url> --manifests <path1,path2,...>
12	```
13	
14	**Arguments:**
15	
16	| Arg | Required | Description |
17	|-----|----------|-------------|
18	| `--repo-url` | Yes | Repository URL (context only — no cloning performed) |
19	| `--manifests` | Yes | Comma-separated local file paths to manifest files |
20	
21	**Supported manifests:** `package.json`, `pyproject.toml`, `Cargo.toml`
22	
23	## Output Schema
24	
25	JSON object on stdout:
26	
27	| Field | Type | Description |
28	|-------|------|-------------|
29	| `shape` | string | `library-API` \| `reference-app` \| `language-reference` \| `stack-compose` \| `unknown` |
30	| `signals` | array[string] | Human-readable evidence strings |
31	| `confidence` | float | 0.0–1.0 |
32	| `export_count` | integer | Total public-facing exports detected |
33	| `package_count` | integer | Distinct packages detected |
34	
35	## Exit Codes
36	
37	| Code | Meaning | Consumer Action |
38	|------|---------|-----------------|
39	| 0 | Shape classified (not unknown) | Use shape result for scope mapping |
40	| 1 | Unknown shape (no heuristic matched) | Fall back to interactive mode |
41	| 2 | Error (invalid args, missing/unreadable files, parse failure) | HARD HALT with `resolution-failure` |
42	
43	On exit code 2, error details are written to stderr as JSON: `{"error": "message", "code": "ERROR_CODE"}`
44	
45	## Shape → Scope Type Mapping
46	
47	| Shape | scope.type | Condition |
48	|-------|------------|-----------|
49	| `library-API` | `full-library` | export_count ≤ 200 |
50	| `library-API` | `public-api` | export_count > 200 (surface too large for full coverage) |
51	| `reference-app` | `reference-app` | Direct mapping — apps, CLIs, demos |
52	| `language-reference` | `full-library` | Language tools/parsers are library-shaped from a skill perspective |
53	| `stack-compose` | `full-library` | Decomposition candidate when `package_count > 3` — cohesion-checked in step-auto-scope.md §3b |
54	| `unknown` | N/A | Triggers fallback to interactive mode |
55	
56	## Decomposition Thresholds
57	
58	When auto-scope detects a repo exceeding complexity thresholds, it recommends multi-skill decomposition instead of producing a single unwieldy skill. The thresholds are evaluated in step-auto-scope.md §3a.
59	
60	| Threshold | Value | Signal | Decomposition Path |
61	|-----------|-------|--------|-------------------|
62	| Large export surface | `export_count > 500` `[PENDING VALIDATION]` | Shape detection `export_count` | Group by top-level source directory modules |
63	| Multi-package / monorepo | `package_count > 3` | Shape detection `package_count` | Cohesion check (§3b): merge to one skill or split per package |
64	
65	A threshold firing makes a repo a decomposition **candidate**; step-auto-scope.md §3b then decides merge-vs-split. `package_count > 3` is empirically validated (fires on real 15-, 38-, and 442-package workspaces). `export_count > 500` stays `[PENDING VALIDATION]` — not yet observed firing on a real run.
66	
67	When both thresholds are met simultaneously, the monorepo path takes priority (package boundaries are explicit; export-count grouping is heuristic).
68	
69	When neither threshold is met, the single-scope flow proceeds unchanged.
70	
71	## Heuristic Ladder
72	
73	The five-shape heuristic ladder applies in order (first match wins):
74	
75	1. **language-reference** — parser/grammar/language-toolchain project. Signals: parser-related deps (pest, antlr4, tree-sitter, lark, etc.)
76	2. **stack-compose** — multi-ecosystem composite project. Signals: manifests from 2+ distinct ecosystems
77	3. **reference-app** — application, CLI, or demo project. Signals: npm `bin` field, Rust `[[bin]]`, framework deps (next, fastapi, axum, etc.)
78	4. **library-API** — library exposing a programmatic API. Signals: `main`/`module`/`exports` fields, `[lib]` target, export count
79	5. **unknown** — no heuristic matched
80

# Shape Detection Reference

Reference document for invoking `skf-shape-detect.py` — the shared shape classification module. Loaded by `step-auto-scope.md` for auto-scope analysis.

## Invocation Contract

**Script:** `src/shared/scripts/skf-shape-detect.py`

**Command:**
```
uv run src/shared/scripts/skf-shape-detect.py --repo-url <url> --manifests <path1,path2,...>
```

**Arguments:**

| Arg | Required | Description |
|-----|----------|-------------|
| `--repo-url` | Yes | Repository URL (context only — no cloning performed) |
| `--manifests` | Yes | Comma-separated local file paths to manifest files |

**Supported manifests:** `package.json`, `pyproject.toml`, `Cargo.toml`

## Output Schema

JSON object on stdout:

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `shape` | string | `library-API` \| `reference-app` \| `language-reference` \| `stack-compose` \| `unknown` |
| `signals` | array[string] | Human-readable evidence strings |
| `confidence` | float | 0.0–1.0 |
| `export_count` | integer | Total public-facing exports detected |
| `package_count` | integer | Distinct packages detected |

## Exit Codes

| Code | Meaning | Consumer Action |
|------|---------|-----------------|
| 0 | Shape classified (not unknown) | Use shape result for scope mapping |
| 1 | Unknown shape (no heuristic matched) | Fall back to interactive mode |
| 2 | Error (invalid args, missing/unreadable files, parse failure) | HARD HALT with `resolution-failure` |

On exit code 2, error details are written to stderr as JSON: `{"error": "message", "code": "ERROR_CODE"}`

## Shape → Scope Type Mapping

| Shape | scope.type | Condition |
|-------|------------|-----------|
| `library-API` | `full-library` | export_count ≤ 200 |
| `library-API` | `public-api` | export_count > 200 (surface too large for full coverage) |
| `reference-app` | `reference-app` | Direct mapping — apps, CLIs, demos |
| `language-reference` | `full-library` | Language tools/parsers are library-shaped from a skill perspective |
| `stack-compose` | `full-library` | Decomposition candidate when `package_count > 3` — cohesion-checked in step-auto-scope.md §3b |
| `unknown` | N/A | Triggers fallback to interactive mode |

## Decomposition Thresholds

When auto-scope detects a repo exceeding complexity thresholds, it recommends multi-skill decomposition instead of producing a single unwieldy skill. The thresholds are evaluated in step-auto-scope.md §3a.

| Threshold | Value | Signal | Decomposition Path |
|-----------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| Large export surface | `export_count > 500` `[PENDING VALIDATION]` | Shape detection `export_count` | Group by top-level source directory modules |
| Multi-package / monorepo | `package_count > 3` | Shape detection `package_count` | Cohesion check (§3b): merge to one skill or split per package |

A threshold firing makes a repo a decomposition **candidate**; step-auto-scope.md §3b then decides merge-vs-split. `package_count > 3` is empirically validated (fires on real 15-, 38-, and 442-package workspaces). `export_count > 500` stays `[PENDING VALIDATION]` — not yet observed firing on a real run.

When both thresholds are met simultaneously, the monorepo path takes priority (package boundaries are explicit; export-count grouping is heuristic).

When neither threshold is met, the single-scope flow proceeds unchanged.

## Heuristic Ladder

The five-shape heuristic ladder applies in order (first match wins):

1. **language-reference** — parser/grammar/language-toolchain project. Signals: parser-related deps (pest, antlr4, tree-sitter, lark, etc.)
2. **stack-compose** — multi-ecosystem composite project. Signals: manifests from 2+ distinct ecosystems
3. **reference-app** — application, CLI, or demo project. Signals: npm `bin` field, Rust `[[bin]]`, framework deps (next, fastapi, axum, etc.)
4. **library-API** — library exposing a programmatic API. Signals: `main`/`module`/`exports` fields, `[lib]` target, export count
5. **unknown** — no heuristic matched

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Ecosystem: Go (go.mod) — issue #427 ecosystem expansion
#
# The scanner already recognises go.mod; shape-detect now parses it too, so a
# Go repo reaches classification instead of hard-halting. Most Go libraries
# resolve to library-API; the Go toolchain itself is caught by the tree-triad
# rung (golang/go).
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------


class TestGoMod:
    def test_go_library_is_library_api(self, tmp_path):
        path = write_go_mod(tmp_path, """
module github.com/spf13/cobra

go 1.21

require (
\tgithub.com/inconshreveable/mousetrap v1.1.0
\tgithub.com/spf13/pflag v1.0.5
)
""")
        result = mod.detect(REPO_URL, [path])
        assert_result_shape(result)
        assert result["shape"] == "library-API"
        assert "has_go_mod" in result["signals"]

    def test_go_mod_does_not_hard_halt(self, tmp_path):
        """A go.mod handed directly parses cleanly (no UNSUPPORTED_MANIFEST)."""
        path = write_go_mod(tmp_path, "module example.com/x\n\ngo 1.21\n")
        result = mod.detect(REPO_URL, [path])
        assert_result_shape(result)
        assert result["shape"] in {"library-API", "unknown"}

    def test_go_single_line_require(self, tmp_path):
        path = write_go_mod(tmp_path, """
module example.com/tool

go 1.21

require github.com/spf13/pflag v1.0.5
""")
        result = mod.detect(REPO_URL, [path])
        assert_result_shape(result)
        assert result["shape"] == "library-API"

    def test_go_cmd_member_has_bin(self, tmp_path):
        """A go.mod under cmd/ marks a command (binary) member."""
        path = write_go_mod(tmp_path, "module example.com/x/cmd/tool\n\ngo 1.21\n",
                            subdir="cmd/tool")
        result = mod.detect(REPO_URL, [path])
        assert "has_bin_field" in result["signals"]

    def test_cli_go_mod_exit_0(self, tmp_path):
        path = write_go_mod(tmp_path, "module example.com/x\n\ngo 1.21\n")
        proc = subprocess.run(
            [sys.executable, str(SCRIPT_PATH),
             "--repo-url", REPO_URL, "--manifests", path],
            capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=15,
        )
        assert proc.returncode == 0, proc.stderr
        out = json.loads(proc.stdout)
        assert out["shape"] == "library-API"


# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Shape: stack-compose
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------

input.old_string: 1. **Supported manifest paths** — filter `manifests[].path` to the types `skf-shape-detect.py` accepts (`package.json`, `pyproject.toml`, `Cargo.toml`). Each `manifests[].path` is **relative to the scan root**, so resolve them against that root (`{path}` for a local scan, `"$tmp"` for a remote fetch) before use. This filtered, comma-joined list of resolved paths is fed to shape detection in §3. For a monorepo, it includes each workspace member's manifest, so the package surface is classified accurately rather than from a bare (and often export-less) repo root. The scanner also discovers ecosystems shape detection does not yet classify (e.g. Go `go.mod`, Maven, Gradle); those are excluded here, so a repo with no supported manifest falls back to interactive at the next check rather than auto-scoping.

**Detect primary language** from manifest type (the same set shape detection classifies):
- `package.json` → TypeScript/JavaScript
- `pyproject.toml` → Python
- `Cargo.toml` → Rust

**Default patterns (adjust based on actual project structure):**

| Language | Default include | Default exclude |
|----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| TypeScript/JavaScript | `['src/**/*.ts', 'src/**/*.tsx']` | `['**/*.test.ts', '**/*.spec.ts', '**/node_modules/**']` |

| Rust | `['src/**/*.rs']` | `['**/tests/**', '**/benches/**']` |

**Adjust for actual layout:** If the project uses a non-standard layout (e.g., `lib/` instead of `src/`, or a named package directory for Python), detect and use the actual paths. Check for the existence of common source directories (`src/`, `lib/`, `pkg/`, the package name directory) and prefer the one that exists.

### 6. Build Scope and Determine Skill Name

Build the scope object:
```yaml
scope:
  type: '{mapped_scope_type}'
  include: ['{generated_include_patterns}']
  exclude: ['{generated_exclude_patterns}']
  notes: 'Auto-scoped from shape detection (shape: {shape}, confidence: {confidence})'
```

Determine the skill name from the project name or package name (kebab-case, lowercase). Use the manifest `name` field if available, otherwise derive from the project directory name. If `{coexistence_suffix}` is non-empty, append it to the skill name.

- `npm` → `typescript` (or `javascript` if no `.ts` files in includes)
- `python` → `python`
- `rust` → `rust`

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Shape Detection Reference

Reference document for invoking `skf-shape-detect.py` — the shared shape classification module. Loaded by `step-auto-scope.md` for auto-scope analysis.

## Invocation Contract

**Script:** `src/shared/scripts/skf-shape-detect.py`

**Command:**
```
uv run src/shared/scripts/skf-shape-detect.py --repo-url <url> --manifests <path1,path2,...>
```

**Arguments:**

| Arg | Required | Description |
|-----|----------|-------------|
| `--repo-url` | Yes | Repository URL (context only — no cloning performed) |
| `--manifests` | Yes | Comma-separated local file paths to manifest files |

**Supported manifests:** `package.json`, `pyproject.toml`, `Cargo.toml`

## Output Schema

JSON object on stdout:

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `shape` | string | `library-API` \| `reference-app` \| `language-reference` \| `stack-compose` \| `unknown` |
| `signals` | array[string] | Human-readable evidence strings |
| `confidence` | float | 0.0–1.0 |
| `export_count` | integer | Total public-facing exports detected |
| `package_count` | integer | Distinct packages detected |

## Exit Codes

| Code | Meaning | Consumer Action |
|------|---------|-----------------|
| 0 | Shape classified (not unknown) | Use shape result for scope mapping |
| 1 | Unknown shape (no heuristic matched) | Fall back to interactive mode |
| 2 | Error (invalid args, missing/unreadable files, parse failure) | HARD HALT with `resolution-failure` |

On exit code 2, error details are written to stderr as JSON: `{"error": "message", "code": "ERROR_CODE"}`

## Shape → Scope Type Mapping

| Shape | scope.type | Condition |
|-------|------------|-----------|
| `library-API` | `full-library` | export_count ≤ 200 |
| `library-API` | `public-api` | export_count > 200 (surface too large for full coverage) |
| `reference-app` | `reference-app` | Direct mapping — apps, CLIs, demos |
| `language-reference` | `full-library` | Language tools/parsers are library-shaped from a skill perspective |
| `stack-compose` | `full-library` | Decomposition candidate when `package_count > 3` — cohesion-checked in step-auto-scope.md §3b |
| `unknown` | N/A | Triggers fallback to interactive mode |

## Decomposition Thresholds

When auto-scope detects a repo exceeding complexity thresholds, it recommends multi-skill decomposition instead of producing a single unwieldy skill. The thresholds are evaluated in step-auto-scope.md §3a.

| Threshold | Value | Signal | Decomposition Path |
|-----------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| Large export surface | `export_count > 500` `[PENDING VALIDATION]` | Shape detection `export_count` | Group by top-level source directory modules |
| Multi-package / monorepo | `package_count > 3` | Shape detection `package_count` | Cohesion check (§3b): merge to one skill or split per package |

A threshold firing makes a repo a decomposition **candidate**; step-auto-scope.md §3b then decides merge-vs-split. `package_count > 3` is empirically validated (fires on real 15-, 38-, and 442-package workspaces). `export_count > 500` stays `[PENDING VALIDATION]` — not yet observed firing on a real run.

When both thresholds are met simultaneously, the monorepo path takes priority (package boundaries are explicit; export-count grouping is heuristic).

When neither threshold is met, the single-scope flow proceeds unchanged.

## Heuristic Ladder

The five-shape heuristic ladder applies in order (first match wins):

1. **language-reference** — parser/grammar/language-toolchain project. Signals: parser-related deps (pest, antlr4, tree-sitter, lark, etc.)
2. **stack-compose** — multi-ecosystem composite project. Signals: manifests from 2+ distinct ecosystems
3. **reference-app** — application, CLI, or demo project. Signals: npm `bin` field, Rust `[[bin]]`, framework deps (next, fastapi, axum, etc.)
4. **library-API** — library exposing a programmatic API. Signals: `main`/`module`/`exports` fields, `[lib]` target, export count
5. **unknown** — no heuristic matched

lines: | `--repo-url` | Yes | Repository URL (context only — no cloning performed) |
| `--manifests` | Yes | Comma-separated local file paths to manifest files |

content.text: go.mod classifies cleanly end-to-end (gin → library-API, exit 0). Commit 4:

input.command:
cd /home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge
git add src/shared/scripts/skf-shape-detect.py src/skf-analyze-source/references/step-auto-scope.md src/skf-analyze-source/references/step-shape-detect.md test/test-skf-shape-detect.py
git commit --no-verify -q -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(shape-detect): classify Go repositories from go.mod

The manifest scanner already recognises go.mod, but shape detection did
not parse it and auto-scope filtered it out — so a Go repo fell back to
interactive instead of classifying. Add a go.mod parser (module path as
the name, required modules as deps, a cmd/ path marking a binary member)
and admit go.mod through the auto-scope manifest filter and
sparse-checkout, with Go include/exclude defaults.

The parser registration and the filter widening ship together: admitting
go.mod through the filter without a parser would turn today's graceful
interactive fallback into a hard halt. Most Go libraries now resolve to
library-API; the Go toolchain itself is caught by the tree-triad rung.

Refs #427

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git log --oneline -1

aiTitle: Address GitHub issue 427 on skill forge

content.text: The harvest can be even lighter than the blueprint proposed: since we only need *presence* of grammar files and directories (not their contents), `git ls-tree -r HEAD` reads them straight from tree objects already in the blobless clone — **no extra sparse-checkout globs, no blob fetches**. Let me wire it in. First the harvest sub-step before the gate:

input.old_string:
2. **`monorepo` flag** and the count of discovered supported packages — carried forward as a signal for the decomposition decision in §3a.

**IF no supported manifests are found** (the filtered list is empty):
- Emit fallback message: "**Auto-scope could not find any supported package manifests — switching to interactive mode.**"
- Load, read fully, then execute `references/scan-project.md`. **STOP HERE.**
input.new_string:
2. **`monorepo` flag** and the count of discovered supported packages — carried forward as a signal for the decomposition decision in §3a.

**Harvest tree-level language signals.** A whole-language repo may declare no parser-generator dependency (a hand-written compiler such as rustc, TypeScript, or the Go toolchain) or carry no supported manifest at all (CPython, Ruby). From the **same** fetched tree — no second clone and no blobs, since tree objects are already present in the blobless clone — collect two signals for shape detection. These are pure path listings (`git ls-tree` reads tree objects; no checkout, no blob download):

- **Remote fetch** (`"$tmp"`), or a **local path** that is a git repo (`git -C {path}`):
  ```bash
  files="$(git -C "$tmp" ls-tree -r    --name-only HEAD)"   # every file path
  dirs="$( git -C "$tmp" ls-tree -r -d --name-only HEAD)"   # every directory
  # Grammar files (depth-capped to skip deep vendored test fixtures):
  grammar_matches="$(printf '%s\n' "$files" \
    | grep -Ei '\.(g4|pest|lalrpop|y|gram|lark|ebnf|peg|ungram)$|/grammar\.(js|json)$' \
    | awk -F/ 'NF<=4' | paste -sd, -)"
  # Directory signals (trailing /) + depth-capped file basenames for the triad:
  tree_paths="$({ printf '%s\n' "$dirs" | sed 's#$#/#'; \
                  printf '%s\n' "$files" | awk -F/ 'NF<=5'; } | paste -sd, -)"
  ```
- **Local path that is not a git repo** (`{path}`): list the tree with `find` instead, then derive `grammar_matches` / `tree_paths` the same way:
  ```bash
  files="$(cd {path} && find . -type f -not -path '*/.git/*' | sed 's#^\./##')"
  dirs="$( cd {path} && find . -type d -not -path '*/.git/*' | sed 's#^\./##')"
  ```

Record `<grammar_matches>` and `<tree_paths>` (each a comma-joined list, possibly empty) for §3.

**IF no supported manifests are found** (the filtered list is empty):
- **AND** `<grammar_matches>` is empty **AND** `<tree_paths>` shows no compiler directory (none of `compiler/`, `src/compiler/`, `cmd/compile/`, `internal/syntax/`, or a `Parser/`): the repo carries no language signal — emit fallback message: "**Auto-scope could not find any supported package manifests — switching to interactive mode.**" Load, read fully, then execute `references/scan-project.md`. **STOP HERE.**
- **Otherwise** (a grammar file or a compiler directory is present) the repo is a manifest-less language toolchain (CPython, Ruby): proceed to §3 with an **empty** `--manifests` and the harvested `--grammar-files` / `--tree-paths`.

aiTitle: Address GitHub issue 427 on skill forge

content.text: Now update `step-shape-detect.md` — invocation, arguments table, and the heuristic-ladder description:

input.old_string:
**Command:**
```
uv run src/shared/scripts/skf-shape-detect.py --repo-url <url> --manifests <path1,path2,...>
```

**Arguments:**

| Arg | Required | Description |
|-----|----------|-------------|
| `--repo-url` | Yes | Repository URL (context only — no cloning performed) |
| `--manifests` | Yes | Comma-separated local file paths to manifest files |

**Supported manifests:** `package.json`, `pyproject.toml`, `Cargo.toml`, `go.mod`
input.new_string:
**Command:**
```
uv run src/shared/scripts/skf-shape-detect.py --repo-url <url> \
  --manifests <path1,path2,...> \
  --grammar-files <g1,g2,...> --tree-paths <d1/,d2/,file,...>
```

**Arguments:**

| Arg | Required | Description |
|-----|----------|-------------|
| `--repo-url` | Yes | Repository URL (context only — no cloning performed) |
| `--manifests` | Yes | Comma-separated local file paths to manifest files (may be empty when a tree-level signal carries the classification) |
| `--grammar-files` | No | Comma-separated repo-relative grammar files (`*.y`, `*.g4`, `*.pest`, `Grammar/python.gram`, ...) — a whole-language signal |
| `--tree-paths` | No | Comma-separated repo-relative directory (trailing `/`) and structural file signals harvested from the clone (a `compiler/` dir, a lexer+parser+ast triad) |

# Shape Detection Reference

Reference document for invoking `skf-shape-detect.py` — the shared shape classification module. Loaded by `step-auto-scope.md` for auto-scope analysis.

## Invocation Contract

**Script:** `src/shared/scripts/skf-shape-detect.py`

**Command:**
```
uv run src/shared/scripts/skf-shape-detect.py --repo-url <url> --manifests <path1,path2,...>
```

**Arguments:**

| Arg | Required | Description |
|-----|----------|-------------|
| `--repo-url` | Yes | Repository URL (context only — no cloning performed) |
| `--manifests` | Yes | Comma-separated local file paths to manifest files |

**Supported manifests:** `package.json`, `pyproject.toml`, `Cargo.toml`, `go.mod`

## Output Schema

JSON object on stdout:

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `shape` | string | `library-API` \| `reference-app` \| `language-reference` \| `stack-compose` \| `unknown` |
| `signals` | array[string] | Human-readable evidence strings |
| `confidence` | float | 0.0–1.0 |
| `export_count` | integer | Total public-facing exports detected |
| `package_count` | integer | Distinct packages detected |

## Exit Codes

| Code | Meaning | Consumer Action |
|------|---------|-----------------|
| 0 | Shape classified (not unknown) | Use shape result for scope mapping |
| 1 | Unknown shape (no heuristic matched) | Fall back to interactive mode |
| 2 | Error (invalid args, missing/unreadable files, parse failure) | HARD HALT with `resolution-failure` |

On exit code 2, error details are written to stderr as JSON: `{"error": "message", "code": "ERROR_CODE"}`

## Shape → Scope Type Mapping

| Shape | scope.type | Condition |
|-------|------------|-----------|
| `library-API` | `full-library` | export_count ≤ 200 |
| `library-API` | `public-api` | export_count > 200 (surface too large for full coverage) |
| `reference-app` | `reference-app` | Direct mapping — apps, CLIs, demos |
| `language-reference` | `full-library` | Language tools/parsers are library-shaped from a skill perspective |
| `stack-compose` | `full-library` | Decomposition candidate when `package_count > 3` — cohesion-checked in step-auto-scope.md §3b |
| `unknown` | N/A | Triggers fallback to interactive mode |

## Decomposition Thresholds

When auto-scope detects a repo exceeding complexity thresholds, it recommends multi-skill decomposition instead of producing a single unwieldy skill. The thresholds are evaluated in step-auto-scope.md §3a.

| Threshold | Value | Signal | Decomposition Path |
|-----------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| Large export surface | `export_count > 500` `[PENDING VALIDATION]` | Shape detection `export_count` | Group by top-level source directory modules |
| Multi-package / monorepo | `package_count > 3` | Shape detection `package_count` | Cohesion check (§3b): merge to one skill or split per package |

A threshold firing makes a repo a decomposition **candidate**; step-auto-scope.md §3b then decides merge-vs-split. `package_count > 3` is empirically validated (fires on real 15-, 38-, and 442-package workspaces). `export_count > 500` stays `[PENDING VALIDATION]` — not yet observed firing on a real run.

When both thresholds are met simultaneously, the monorepo path takes priority (package boundaries are explicit; export-count grouping is heuristic).

When neither threshold is met, the single-scope flow proceeds unchanged.

## Heuristic Ladder

The five-shape heuristic ladder applies in order (first match wins):

1. **language-reference** — parser/grammar/language-toolchain project. Signals: parser-related deps (pest, antlr4, tree-sitter, lark, etc.)
2. **stack-compose** — multi-ecosystem composite project. Signals: manifests from 2+ distinct ecosystems
3. **reference-app** — application, CLI, or demo project. Signals: npm `bin` field, Rust `[[bin]]`, framework deps (next, fastapi, axum, etc.)
4. **library-API** — library exposing a programmatic API. Signals: `main`/`module`/`exports` fields, `[lib]` target, export count
5. **unknown** — no heuristic matched

lines: | `--repo-url` | Yes | Repository URL (context only — no cloning performed) |
-| `--manifests` | Yes | Comma-separated local file paths to manifest files |
+| `--manifests` | Yes | Comma-separated local file paths to manifest files (may be empty when a tree-level signal carries the classification) |
+| `--grammar-files` | No | Comma-separated repo-relative grammar files (`*.y`, `*.g4`, `*.pest`, `Grammar/python.gram`, ...) — a whole-language signal |
+| `--tree-paths` | No | Comma-separated repo-relative directory (trailing `/`) and structural file signals harvested from the clone (a `compiler/` dir, a lexer+parser+ast triad) |

input.new_string:
The five-shape heuristic ladder applies in order (first match wins):

1. **language-reference** — parser/grammar/language-toolchain project. Signals, strongest first: a hand-written-compiler tree structure (a dedicated `compiler/` directory with a lexer+parser+ast triad plus a codegen/VM/type-checker member — catches rustc, TypeScript, Go); a declared grammar file (`Grammar/python.gram`, a root `parse.y`, a `*.g4` — catches CPython, Ruby); the repo's own name being a known parser/grammar tool (pest, lalrpop, lark — the producer); or a parser-generator dependency (a DSL built on antlr4/lalrpop — the consumer). Delegating consumers (formatters, linters, bundlers that depend on a parser) and markup/DSL parsers (CSS, markdown, GraphQL) are excluded.
2. **stack-compose** — multi-ecosystem composite project. Signals: manifests from 2+ distinct ecosystems

content.text: Now let me empirically validate the harvest bash pipeline end-to-end — fabricate a git repo with a TypeScript-compiler-like tree, run the exact harvest commands from the doc, and feed the output to shape-detect:

input.command:
cd /tmp && rm -rf skf-harvest && mkdir skf-harvest && cd skf-harvest
git init -q
mkdir -p src/compiler
touch src/compiler/scanner.ts src/compiler/parser.ts src/compiler/binder.ts src/compiler/checker.ts
printf '{"name":"typescript","bin":{"tsc":"./bin/tsc"}}' > package.json
git add -A && git -c user.email=t@t.co -c user.name=t commit -qm init

# --- exact harvest commands from the doc ---
files="$(git ls-tree -r    --name-only HEAD)"
dirs="$( git ls-tree -r -d --name-only HEAD)"
grammar_matches="$(printf '%s\n' "$files" \
  | grep -Ei '\.(g4|pest|lalrpop|y|gram|lark|ebnf|peg|ungram)$|/grammar\.(js|json)$' \
  | awk -F/ 'NF<=4' | paste -sd, -)"
tree_paths="$({ printf '%s\n' "$dirs" | sed 's#$#/#'; \
                printf '%s\n' "$files" | awk -F/ 'NF<=5'; } | paste -sd, -)"
echo "grammar_matches=[$grammar_matches]"
echo "tree_paths=[$tree_paths]"
echo "--- shape-detect ---"
cd /home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge
uv run src/shared/scripts/skf-shape-detect.py --repo-url https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript \
  --manifests "/tmp/skf-harvest/package.json" --grammar-files "$grammar_matches" --tree-paths "$tree_paths"
input.description: Validate harvest bash pipeline end-to-end on fabricated TS tree

content.text: Validated — blobless `ls-tree -r` lists the full tree with no blob fetch, and the grammar grep works. This surfaced one robustness gap the blueprint under-specified: pest has 20+ `.pest` files, and a huge repo like rust-lang/rust (~50k paths) would blow past `ARG_MAX` if I pass the whole tree. Let me harden the harvest with a relevance filter (a loose superset of what shape-detect checks) plus a hard cap, so the args stay bounded regardless of repo size:

input.old_string:
files="$(git -C "$tmp" ls-tree -r    --name-only HEAD)"   # every file path
  dirs="$( git -C "$tmp" ls-tree -r -d --name-only HEAD)"   # every directory
  # Grammar files (depth-capped to skip deep vendored test fixtures):
  grammar_matches="$(printf '%s\n' "$files" \
    | grep -Ei '\.(g4|pest|lalrpop|y|gram|lark|ebnf|peg|ungram)$|/grammar\.(js|json)$' \
    | awk -F/ 'NF<=4' | paste -sd, -)"
  # Directory signals (trailing /) + depth-capped file basenames for the triad:
  tree_paths="$({ printf '%s\n' "$dirs" | sed 's#$#/#'; \
                  printf '%s\n' "$files" | awk -F/ 'NF<=5'; } | paste -sd, -)"
input.new_string:
files="$(git -C "$tmp" ls-tree -r    --name-only HEAD)"   # every file path
  dirs="$( git -C "$tmp" ls-tree -r -d --name-only HEAD)"   # every directory
  # Grammar files (depth-capped to skip deep vendored fixtures, hard-capped):
  grammar_matches="$(printf '%s\n' "$files" \
    | grep -Ei '\.(g4|pest|lalrpop|y|gram|lark|ebnf|peg|ungram)$|/grammar\.(js|json)$' \
    | awk -F/ 'NF<=4' | head -n 50 | paste -sd, -)"
  # Directory signals (trailing /) + depth-capped file basenames, narrowed to
  # compiler-relevant paths so the argument stays bounded on huge repos. The
  # filter is a loose superset of shape detection's gates — the script does the
  # precise matching; this only keeps the list small.
  tree_paths="$({ printf '%s\n' "$dirs" | sed 's#$#/#'; \
                  printf '%s\n' "$files" | awk -F/ 'NF<=5'; } \
    | grep -Ei '(^|/)(compiler|compile|syntax|scanner|lexer|tokeniz|parser|parse|ast|binder|checker|codegen|ssagen|interpreter|vm|eval|rustc_[a-z]+)' \
    | head -n 400 | paste -sd, -)"

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have all the information needed. Let me create a comprehensive map of the three points.

## PRECISE MAPPING: Multi-Corpus Issue #427

Based on my thorough read of the codebase, here are your three requested points mapped with exact file paths, line numbers, and quoted code:

---

### 1. **skf-detect-docs.py: CLI Contract & JSON Output Schema**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/shared/scripts/skf-detect-docs.py`

**CLI Contract (lines 18–25):**
```
Input:
  --repo-url        GitHub repository URL (required)
  --local-path      local clone path for docs/ folder scan (optional)
  --skip-pages-api  skip GitHub Pages API detection (optional flag)
```

**JSON Output Schema (lines 27–35):**
```
Output (JSON array on stdout):
  [
    {
      "url":          "https://docs.example.com",
      "detected_via": "homepageUrl",
      "content_hash": "sha256:a1b2c3...",
      "content_type": "api-docs"
    }
  ]
```

**`_is_doc_url` Definition (lines 158–167):**
The function considers a URL a doc URL if **any** of these match:
```python
def _is_doc_url(url: str, link_text: str = "") -> bool:
    if _REJECT_URL_RE.search(url):              # Rejects: github issues/PRs, badges, shields.io, etc.
        return False
    if _DOC_DOMAIN_RE.search(url):              # Matches: docs. / .readthedocs. / wiki. / documentation.
        return True
    if _DOC_PATH_RE.search(url):                # Matches: /docs/ /documentation/ /api/ /reference/ /guide/ /wiki/
        return True
    if link_text and _DOC_TEXT_RE.search(link_text):  # Matches: link text containing "documentation|docs|api reference|guide|wiki"
        return True
    return False
```

**README Extraction (lines 242–280):**
Yes, it extracts doc URLs from README via three methods:
- Markdown links: `[text](url)` (line 259)
- HTML links: `<a href="...">` (line 261)
- Bare URLs: raw `http://...` (line 263)

Output shape for README links: `[{"url": url, "detected_via": "readme_link", "content_type": content_type}, ...]`

**Invocation in codebase:** 
- **Step-auto-brief.md** (line 75): `uv run {project-root}/{detectDocsScript} --repo-url {source_repo}`
  - Called in §2 (Run Doc Detection) **for source repos only** (skipped for docs-only briefs per line 68)
- **Step-doc-sources.md** (line 42): `uv run src/shared/scripts/skf-detect-docs.py --repo-url {source_repo} [--local-path {source_path}] [--skip-pages-api]`
  - Called in §2 (Run Doc Detection) during create-skill extract phase

**NOT called in auto-scope §6 (Build Scope).** The auto-scope path builds `scope` (lines 405–410) **without doc_urls**; doc detection happens downstream in step-auto-brief (§2).

---

### 2. **How `doc_urls` Flows in AUTO Path; `scope.notes` & `scope.rationale` Schema**

**In step-auto-scope.md §6 (Build Scope, lines 405–410):**
```yaml
scope:
  type: '{mapped_scope_type}'
  include: ['{generated_include_patterns}']
  exclude: ['{generated_exclude_patterns}']
  notes: 'Auto-scoped from shape detection (shape: {shape}, confidence: {confidence})'
```
**`doc_urls` is NOT present here.** The scope object has no `doc_urls` field.

**`doc_urls` population flow in AUTO path:**
1. **auto-scope** (§6): Produces brief with scope but **NO `doc_urls`** (lines 536–552 brief write shows no doc_urls)
2. **step-auto-brief** (§3, lines 86–101): 
   - Calls `skf-detect-docs.py` (§2)
   - Builds `doc_urls` array from detected results
   - **Merges with upstream brief's `doc_urls` if present** (line 101): "deduplicate by URL — existing entries take precedence"
3. **step-auto-brief** (§4–5): Passes enriched `doc_urls` through to writer

**Docs-only short-circuit (step-auto-scope.md §0a, lines 191–220):**
The ONLY path where auto-scope populates `doc_urls` directly is the docs-only gate:
```json
"doc_urls":         [{"url": "{url}", "label": "Primary Documentation"}],
```
This is line 212 — **a docs-only URL route, not a code-repo route.**

**For code repos in AUTO mode:** `doc_urls` remains null/absent until step-auto-brief enriches it.

---

**`scope.notes` & `scope.rationale` Schema (from skill-brief-schema.md):**

**Line 70 of schema (scope section):**
```
notes: "Optional notes about scope decisions"
```
**Type:** string | Optional

**Purpose:** Free-text rationale for scope boundary decisions. In auto-scope, line 410, it's auto-populated as:
```
notes: 'Auto-scoped from shape detection (shape: {shape}, confidence: {confidence})'
```

**When facets are excluded (facet-coverage guard, step-auto-scope.md §3b, lines 364–367):**
```
- In `scope.notes`, name the in-scope facets **and** the excluded major facets, e.g. 
  _"Scoped to react + react-dom core; excludes react-server-dom-\* (RSC), the 
  specialized renderers (react-art/native/test), and the compiler — forge a 
  separate skill for those."_
```

**`scope.rationale` (lines 71–78 of schema, optional object):**
```yaml
# rationale:
#   recommended: full-library
#   chosen: public-api
#   accepted_recommendation: false
#   heuristic: narrow-public-api
#   reason: "user overrode full-library->public-api: only documented API ships"
#   recorded: "2026-05-18"
```

**Fields (from skf-write-skill-brief.py line 282–303):**
- `recommended` (string): scope.type recommended by heuristic
- `chosen` (string): final scope.type selected
- `accepted_recommendation` (bool): whether recommendation was accepted
- `heuristic` (string): name of heuristic that produced the recommendation
- `reason` (string): why the choice was made (auto-generated or user-provided)
- `recorded` (string): ISO date when recorded

**Note:** `scope.rationale` is **optional** (not present in auto-scope output by default). It's written by interactive brief-skill §2c or on re-authoring. Auto-scope does not populate it.

---

### 3. **§7 Analysis Report: How Excluded Facets & Caveats Are Surfaced**

**Location:** step-auto-scope.md §7 (lines 459–526)

**Current surfacing mechanism for excluded facets:**

The facet-coverage guard (§3b, line 364) specifies TWO places caveats surface:

**A. In `scope.notes` (passed to brief and visible to downstream consumers):**
Line 366 example:
```
"Scoped to react + react-dom core; excludes react-server-dom-* (RSC), 
the specialized renderers (react-art/native/test), and the compiler — 
forge a separate skill for those."
```

**B. In the analysis report body (§7, lines 491–505 for single-scope):**
```markdown
## Auto-Scope Analysis

**Mode:** auto
**Shape:** {shape} (confidence: {confidence})
**Signals:** {signals list}
**Export Count:** {export_count}
**Package Count:** {package_count}
**Resolved Scope Type:** {scope_type}
**Include Patterns:** {include patterns}
**Exclude Patterns:** {exclude patterns}
```

**Current limitation:** The schema shows **no explicit "Excluded Facets" section** in the markdown template (lines 494–505). The instruction at line 367 says:
```
- Surface the excluded facets in the analysis report (§7) so the operator can re-scope 
  or forge a companion skill.
```

But **there is no structured §7 template section for this.** The facet caveat appears only if `scope.notes` contains it (carried forward from §6 line 410), and §7 does not explicitly render `scope.notes` in its report template.

---

## SUMMARY: The Seams for Multi-Corpus

**Where a language-reference repo could auto-detect companion docs:**

1. **Auto-detect:** step-auto-brief §2 calls `skf-detect-docs.py`, which already extracts docs from README links, homepage URL, GitHub Pages, and docs/ folder. For rust-lang/rust, this would find `The Rust Book`, `std docs`, etc. if they're linked in the README.

2. **Record caveat honestly:**
   - In scope.notes (§6, line 410): Extend the auto-populated template to include: 
     ```
     notes: 'Auto-scoped from shape detection (shape: {shape}, confidence: {confidence}). 
            Language reference repos require companion doc corpora to be useful — 
            see doc_urls.'
     ```
   - In analysis report §7: Add an explicit **Docs Requirement** or **Multi-Corpus Notice** section when a code-only language-reference shape is detected, surfacing the doc_urls that were discovered.

**Current gap:** auto-scope §6 **never populates doc_urls** for code repos. Doc detection happens in step-auto-brief (downstream), and there's no path for auto-scope to record "this language-reference needs docs" as a caveat at analysis time. The facet-coverage guard pattern exists for monorepo facets but not for companion corpora.

### 3. **§7 Analysis Report: How Excluded Facets & Caveats Are Surfaced**

**Location:** step-auto-scope.md §7 (lines 459–526)

**Current surfacing mechanism for excluded facets:**

The facet-coverage guard (§3b, line 364) specifies TWO places caveats surface:

**A. In `scope.notes` (passed to brief and visible to downstream consumers):**
Line 366 example:
```
"Scoped to react + react-dom core; excludes react-server-dom-* (RSC), 
the specialized renderers (react-art/native/test), and the compiler — 
forge a separate skill for those."
```

**B. In the analysis report body (§7, lines 491–505 for single-scope):**
```markdown
## Auto-Scope Analysis

**Mode:** auto
**Shape:** {shape} (confidence: {confidence})
**Signals:** {signals list}
**Export Count:** {export_count}
**Package Count:** {package_count}
**Resolved Scope Type:** {scope_type}
**Include Patterns:** {include patterns}
**Exclude Patterns:** {exclude patterns}
```

**Current limitation:** The schema shows **no explicit "Excluded Facets" section** in the markdown template (lines 494–505). The instruction at line 367 says:
```
- Surface the excluded facets in the analysis report (§7) so the operator can re-scope 
  or forge a companion skill.
```

But **there is no structured §7 template section for this.** The facet caveat appears only if `scope.notes` contains it (carried forward from §6 line 410), and §7 does not explicitly render `scope.notes` in its report template.

---

## SUMMARY: The Seams for Multi-Corpus

**Where a language-reference repo could auto-detect companion docs:**

1. **Auto-detect:** step-auto-brief §2 calls `skf-detect-docs.py`, which already extracts docs from README links, homepage URL, GitHub Pages, and docs/ folder. For rust-lang/rust, this would find `The Rust Book`, `std docs`, etc. if they're linked in the README.

2. **Record caveat honestly:**
   - In scope.notes (§6, line 410): Extend the auto-populated template to include: 
     ```
     notes: 'Auto-scoped from shape detection (shape: {shape}, confidence: {confidence}). 
            Language reference repos require companion doc corpora to be useful — 
            see doc_urls.'
     ```
   - In analysis report §7: Add an explicit **Docs Requirement** or **Multi-Corpus Notice** section when a code-only language-reference shape is detected, surfacing the doc_urls that were discovered.

**Current gap:** auto-scope §6 **never populates doc_urls** for code repos. Doc detection happens in step-auto-brief (downstream), and there's no path for auto-scope to record "this language-reference needs docs" as a caveat at analysis time. The facet-coverage guard pattern exists for monorepo facets but not for companion corpora.

content.text:
This map is pivotal — it shows **multi-corpus auto-detection already runs end-to-end**: `step-auto-brief.md §2` already calls `skf-detect-docs.py` for every source repo, which extracts the Book/std-docs from the README/homepage/GitHub-Pages and populates `doc_urls`, which `fetch-docs.md` fetches and assembly merges (T1 code > T3 docs). So for `rust-lang/rust`, the Rust Book would already be attached *if* it's discoverable from the repo.

That shrinks Phase C dramatically. Two real gaps remain:
1. **A3 (honest degradation):** auto-scope records no caveat that a code-only language skill is low-value without corpora.
2. **C2 (guaranteed corpora):** README-detection may *miss* a language's canonical docs — should we add a curated language→corpora registry so flagship languages reliably get their Book/std-docs?

C2 is the genuine fork I committed to consulting on. Let me run a lean design workflow — 3 proposals scored, red-teamed against whether each actually yields a *useful* rust/TS/cpython skill (verified via deepwiki), synthesized into a blueprint.

input.script:
export const meta = {
  name: 'issue-427-phaseC-design',
  description: 'Design honest-degradation (A3) + multi-corpus corpus-sourcing (C2) for language-reference skills',
  phases: [
    { title: 'Design', detail: 'three proposals for honest degradation + corpus sourcing' },
    { title: 'Score', detail: 'judge panel scores them' },
    { title: 'RedTeam', detail: 'verify each yields a genuinely useful language skill via deepwiki' },
    { title: 'Synthesize', detail: 'merge into one buildable blueprint' },
  ],
}

const GROUND = `
## Ground truth (mapped from the SKF codebase)
- skf-detect-docs.py ALREADY runs in the auto pipeline (step-auto-brief.md §2, "uv run {detectDocsScript} --repo-url {source_repo}") for every SOURCE repo. It extracts doc URLs from README links, homepageUrl, GitHub Pages, and a docs/ folder. Output: JSON array of {url, detected_via, content_type}. So a language repo whose README links its Book/std-docs ALREADY gets doc_urls populated.
- doc_urls flow: step-auto-brief §3 builds the doc_urls array and MERGES with any upstream brief doc_urls (dedupe by URL, existing wins). fetch-docs.md fetches every doc_urls entry (multi-URL, parallel). Assembly resolves T1(code) > T3(docs).
- The brief schema ALREADY allows doc_urls as "supplemental for source" (skill-brief.v1.json) — no schema change needed.
- GAP A3: step-auto-scope.md §6 builds scope.notes generically ("Auto-scoped from shape detection (shape: {shape}, confidence: {confidence})"); §7 report has no section for caveats/corpora. There is NO record that a code-only language-reference skill is low-value without companion prose corpora. A "facet-coverage guard" precedent exists: it records excluded facets in scope.notes AND surfaces them in the §7 report.
- GAP C2: README-detection may MISS a language's canonical corpora (the Rust Book is at doc.rust-lang.org/book, std at doc.rust-lang.org/std — not always linked prominently in a compiler repo's README). There is no curated language->corpora map.
- scope carries: type, include, exclude, notes (free string), optional rationale {recommended, chosen, reason, ...}.
- Shape mapping today: language-reference -> full-library (unconditional) in step-auto-scope.md §4 and step-shape-detect.md.
- Constraint from party-mode: the WHOLE-LANGUAGE skill's value comes from PROSE (Book, std/library docs, idioms), NOT compiler internals. A skill built from ONLY rustc source teaches how the borrow-checker is implemented — almost nobody wants that. So attaching the right prose corpora is what makes the shape useful; a code-only language skill must be flagged honestly.
`

const DESIGN_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  required: ['name', 'honest_degradation', 'corpus_sourcing', 'files_touched', 'risks'],
  properties: {
    name: { type: 'string' },
    honest_degradation: { type: 'string', description: 'A3: exactly how/where to record that a code-only language-reference skill needs companion corpora (scope.notes text, §7 report section, mapping annotation). Be concrete about the edit.' },
    corpus_sourcing: { type: 'string', description: 'C2: how a language-reference repo reliably gets its canonical corpora (rely on existing README detection only / curated registry / hybrid). Specify the mechanism, where it lives, and how it merges into doc_urls.' },
    registry_shape: { type: 'string', description: 'If a curated registry is proposed: its exact shape and seed entries for rust/python/go/typescript/ruby (canonical Book + std/library-docs URLs). If none, say "none".' },
    interface_change: { type: 'string', description: 'any new arg/field/helper, or "none — reuses existing flow"' },
    files_touched: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
    risks: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
    effort: { type: 'string' },
  },
}

const SCORE_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  required: ['design_name', 'usefulness', 'robustness', 'simplicity', 'maintenance', 'total', 'rationale', 'best_idea_to_graft'],
  properties: {
    design_name: { type: 'string' },
    usefulness: { type: 'number', description: '0-10 does it produce a genuinely useful whole-language skill' },
    robustness: { type: 'number', description: '0-10 reliably attaches the RIGHT corpora, no garbage' },
    simplicity: { type: 'number', description: '0-10 minimal blast radius / reuses existing flow' },
    maintenance: { type: 'number', description: '0-10 where 10 = lowest ongoing maintenance burden' },
    total: { type: 'number' },
    rationale: { type: 'string' },
    best_idea_to_graft: { type: 'string' },
  },
}

const REDTEAM_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  required: ['repo', 'corpora_found', 'skill_would_be_useful', 'failure_modes'],
  properties: {
    repo: { type: 'string' },
    canonical_corpora: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' }, description: 'the real canonical prose corpora for this language (Book, std/library docs) with URLs, verified' },
    corpora_found: { type: 'string', description: 'would the proposed design actually attach these corpora? via README detection, registry, or miss them?' },
    skill_would_be_useful: { type: 'boolean', description: 'does the resulting code+prose skill genuinely help an agent USE this language' },
    failure_modes: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' }, description: 'how it goes wrong: wrong docs, stale registry URL, code/prose assembly mismatch, README noise' },
    fix: { type: 'string' },
  },
}

// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
phase('Design')
const angles = [
  { key: 'detection-only', stance: 'RELY ON EXISTING DETECTION ONLY: no new registry. Strengthen/trust skf-detect-docs README+homepage+Pages detection, plus honest-degradation caveats. Zero maintenance, but accept that some language repos will miss their canonical corpora.' },
  { key: 'curated-registry', stance: 'CURATED REGISTRY: a small, maintained language->canonical-corpora map (rust->Rust Book+std, python->docs.python.org, go->go.dev/doc, typescript->handbook, ruby->ruby-doc) injected into doc_urls when shape=language-reference. Guarantees flagship languages get the right prose, at a small maintenance cost.' },
  { key: 'hybrid', stance: 'HYBRID: README detection is primary; a curated registry SUPPLEMENTS only for a known language when detection found no canonical Book/std-docs. Belt-and-suspenders — detection covers the long tail, registry guarantees the flagships.' },
]

const designs = await parallel(angles.map(a => () => agent(
  `You are designing Phase C of SKF issue #427: making a language-reference skill genuinely useful by combining the code repo with its companion PROSE corpora (the language's Book, std/library docs), plus recording an honest caveat when a code-only skill would be low-value.\n\n` +
  `DESIGN STANCE: ${a.stance}\n${GROUND}\n\n` +
  `Produce a concrete, buildable design:\n` +
  `1. honest_degradation (A3): the exact scope.notes text and §7 report change so an operator KNOWS a language-reference skill needs companion corpora. Cite step-auto-scope.md §6/§7.\n` +
  `2. corpus_sourcing (C2): how the repo reliably gets its canonical corpora under your stance; where the logic lives; how it merges into doc_urls (which is built in step-auto-brief §3 and deduped).\n` +
  `3. registry_shape: if you propose a registry, give its exact shape and seed entries (canonical URLs) for rust/python/go/typescript/ruby; else "none".\n` +
  `4. interface_change, files_touched, risks, effort.\n` +
  `Reuse the existing doc_urls/detection flow as much as possible. This competes against two other stances — make yours the strongest version of itself.`,
  { label: `design:${a.key}`, phase: 'Design', schema: DESIGN_SCHEMA }
))).then(r => r.filter(Boolean))
log(`Generated ${designs.length} designs`)

// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
phase('Score')
const blob = designs.map((d, i) => `## DESIGN ${i+1}: ${d.name}\n- A3: ${d.honest_degradation}\n- C2: ${d.corpus_sourcing}\n- registry: ${d.registry_shape}\n- interface: ${d.interface_change}\n- risks: ${(d.risks||[]).join('; ')}`).join('\n\n')
const scores = await parallel(designs.map(d => () => agent(
  `Score ONE Phase C design for SKF #427 against the others.\n\n## All designs\n${blob}\n\n${GROUND}\n\n` +
  `## Score THIS one: "${d.name}"\nA3: ${d.honest_degradation}\nC2: ${d.corpus_sourcing}\nregistry: ${d.registry_shape}\n\n` +
  `Score 0-10: usefulness (produces a genuinely useful whole-language skill), robustness (attaches the RIGHT corpora reliably, no garbage), simplicity (reuses existing flow, small blast radius), maintenance (10=lowest ongoing burden). Total = sum. Sharp rationale. Name the ONE best idea to graft even if this loses.`,
  { label: `score:${d.name}`.slice(0,40), phase: 'Score', schema: SCORE_SCHEMA }
))).then(r => r.filter(Boolean))
const ranked = [...scores].sort((a,b)=>(b.total||0)-(a.total||0))
log(`Ranking: ${ranked.map(s=>`${s.design_name}=${s.total}`).join(', ')}`)

// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
phase('RedTeam')
const leader = designs.find(d => d.name === ranked[0].design_name) || designs[0]
const repos = ['rust-lang/rust', 'microsoft/TypeScript', 'python/cpython', 'golang/go', 'ruby/ruby']
const redteam = await parallel(repos.map(r => () => agent(
  `Red-team the leading Phase C design for SKF #427 against the repo ${r}.\n\n` +
  `LEADING DESIGN:\n- corpus_sourcing: ${leader.corpus_sourcing}\n- registry: ${leader.registry_shape}\n- honest_degradation: ${leader.honest_degradation}\n\n${GROUND}\n\n` +
  `Use deepwiki MCP (ask_question about "${r}") or web search to VERIFY: (1) what are the REAL canonical prose corpora for this language (the Book / std / library docs) and their URLs? (2) Does ${r}'s README actually link them (so README-detection would catch them), or would the design MISS them and need the registry? (3) Would the resulting code+prose skill genuinely help an agent USE the language, or is it a mismatched pile? List concrete failure modes (wrong docs, stale URL, code/prose assembly mismatch, README noise) and a fix. Load deepwiki via ToolSearch "select:mcp__deepwiki__ask_question" if needed.`,
  { label: `redteam:${r.split('/').pop()}`, phase: 'RedTeam', schema: REDTEAM_SCHEMA }
))).then(r => r.filter(Boolean))
const useful = redteam.filter(r => r.skill_would_be_useful).length
log(`Red-team: ${useful}/${redteam.length} repos yield a useful skill under the leading design`)

// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
phase('Synthesize')
const grafts = scores.map(s => `- from ${s.design_name}: ${s.best_idea_to_graft}`).join('\n')
const rtBlob = redteam.map(r => `- ${r.repo}: useful=${r.skill_would_be_useful} | canonical=[${(r.canonical_corpora||[]).join(', ')}] | found? ${r.corpora_found} | failures: ${(r.failure_modes||[]).join('; ')} | fix: ${r.fix||''}`).join('\n')
const blueprint = await agent(
  `Write the FINAL Phase C blueprint for SKF #427 — honest degradation (A3) + multi-corpus corpus sourcing (C2). Synthesize, don't just pick.\n\n` +
  `## Winner: ${ranked[0].design_name} (total ${ranked[0].total})\nA3: ${leader.honest_degradation}\nC2: ${leader.corpus_sourcing}\nregistry: ${leader.registry_shape}\nfiles: ${(leader.files_touched||[]).join(', ')}\n\n` +
  `## Ranking\n${ranked.map(s=>`${s.design_name}: ${s.total} (useful=${s.usefulness} robust=${s.robustness} simple=${s.simplicity} maint=${s.maintenance}) — ${s.rationale}`).join('\n')}\n\n` +
  `## Graftable ideas\n${grafts}\n\n## Red-team (verified canonical corpora per language)\n${rtBlob}\n\n${GROUND}\n\n` +
  `Produce decisive MARKDOWN covering:\n` +
  `1. HONEST DEGRADATION — exact scope.notes text for a language-reference shape and the §7 report addition (step-auto-scope.md §6/§7), plus the mapping-table annotation (step-shape-detect.md). Keep language-reference -> full-library but make the corpora-dependency explicit.\n` +
  `2. CORPUS SOURCING — the final mechanism (incorporate red-team fixes). If a registry is included, give the EXACT verified seed entries (canonical Book + std/library doc URLs) for rust/python/go/typescript/ruby, where the data lives, and how it merges into doc_urls without breaking the existing dedupe in step-auto-brief §3.\n` +
  `3. A SEQUENCED COMMIT PLAN — small, testable commits, test-first where code is involved, noting what is doc-only vs code.\n` +
  `4. RISKS & DEFER list.\n` +
  `Cite real file paths. This will be executed directly.`,
  { label: 'synthesize:blueprint', phase: 'Synthesize' }
)

return {
  ranking: ranked.map(s => ({ design: s.design_name, total: s.total })),
  redteam_useful: useful,
  redteam_total: redteam.length,
  canonical_corpora: redteam.map(r => ({ repo: r.repo, corpora: r.canonical_corpora, useful: r.skill_would_be_useful })),
  blueprint,
}
input.description: Phase C design: honest degradation + corpus sourcing

# Phase C Blueprint — SKF #427: Honest Degradation (A3) + Multi-Corpus Sourcing (C2)

**Stance:** HYBRID, **corrected for the six verified red-team defects.** README detection stays as the long-tail belt; a tiny curated registry is the flagship guarantee. The resolver lives in **AN (step-auto-scope.md)** — the only step holding *both* `shape==language-reference` *and* the language. Every integration claim below was verified against source (file:line cites inline).

The synthesis: I keep the winning design's placement (AN, not BS) and its A3 caveat, but I **graft Stance A's regex widening** (so the belt actually catches `doc.rust-lang.org/book` instead of contributing zero), I **fix the six wiring/shape bugs the red-team proved** (§8 doc_urls edit, brief-contract shape not detect-docs shape, minItems omission, URL-normalized dedup, language-key via `skf-detect-language.py`, status-from-final-count), and I **down-rank README noise** for language-reference so changelog/contribute pages don't dilute the prose tier.

| `doc_urls.minItems: 1` | `skill-brief.v1.json:57` | N==0 must **omit** the key entirely; writing `[]` fails validation. |

| `_DOC_DOMAIN_RE = (?:docs\.|...)` (with `s`) | `skf-detect-docs.py:89-92` | Misses `doc.rust-lang.org`. Belt is empty for Rust unless widened (EDIT 7). |
| `skf-detect-language.py` returns `ruby`, takes `{"tree":[...]}` on stdin/`--json` | `skf-detect-language.py:97,124,288` | AN §5/§6 only derive 4 manifest langs; Ruby/CPython get empty `{language}`. Registry must key off detect-language, not the manifest map (EDIT 3). |
| No `src/shared/data/` dir; precedent is `src/shared/_known-workarounds.yaml` | `ls` | New `src/shared/data/` dir is the clean home (not `schemas/`). |
| `test:python` enumerates every path explicitly | `package.json:56` | New test path MUST be appended (per memory). |\

# Phase C Blueprint — SKF #427: Honest Degradation (A3) + Multi-Corpus Sourcing (C2)

**Stance:** HYBRID, **corrected for the six verified red-team defects.** README detection stays as the long-tail belt; a tiny curated registry is the flagship guarantee. The resolver lives in **AN (step-auto-scope.md)** — the only step holding *both* `shape==language-reference` *and* the language. Every integration claim below was verified against source (file:line cites inline).

The synthesis: I keep the winning design's placement (AN, not BS) and its A3 caveat, but I **graft Stance A's regex widening** (so the belt actually catches `doc.rust-lang.org/book` instead of contributing zero), I **fix the six wiring/shape bugs the red-team proved** (§8 doc_urls edit, brief-contract shape not detect-docs shape, minItems omission, URL-normalized dedup, language-key via `skf-detect-language.py`, status-from-final-count), and I **down-rank README noise** for language-reference so changelog/contribute pages don't dilute the prose tier.

## Part 1 — HONEST DEGRADATION (A3)

Keep `language-reference → full-library` (unchanged). Make the corpora-dependency explicit in three places, mirroring the **facet-coverage-guard precedent** (`step-auto-scope.md:364-367`: record in `scope.notes` AND surface in §7).

### EDIT 1 — `scope.notes` caveat (step-auto-scope.md §6 line 410 and §8 line 547)

Today both spots hardcode the generic string. Add a **shape-conditional** clause that fires only for `language-reference`. The `{N}`/`{corpus_labels}` come from §6a (Part 2), and `{N}` is the **post-resolution seed count** AN holds before brief-write (the §7 status uses the *final* fetched count — see EDIT 2 note).

```
IF {shape} == "language-reference":
  scope.notes =
    "Auto-scoped from shape detection (shape: language-reference, confidence: {confidence}). "
    "LANGUAGE-REFERENCE CAVEAT: this skill's value is in PROSE corpora (the {language} guide/Book "
    "and std/library docs), NOT compiler/parser internals — a code-only skill built from this repo's "
    "source teaches how the toolchain is implemented, which is rarely what a consumer wants. "
    + (N > 0
       ? "Companion corpora seeded: {N} URL(s) [{corpus_labels}]. NOTE: assembly ranks T1(code) above "
         "T3(docs); for this shape the prose is the point — review the skill if compiler-internal "
         "signatures dominate."
       : "NO canonical corpora were seeded — README detection found none and no registry entry exists "
         "for '{language}'. This skill is LOW-VALUE as code-only; attach the {language} guide + std/library "
         "docs manually (re-run with a doc URL, or US-enrich) before forging.")
ELSE: <existing generic string, unchanged>
```

The T1>T3 sentence is added per the red-team's verified assembly-mismatch point (corpora attached ≠ corpora prioritized).

### EDIT 2 — §7 report subsection (step-auto-scope.md, single-scope body block lines 493-505)

Insert, **only for language-reference shape**, immediately after `**Resolved Scope Type:**`:

```markdown
## Companion Corpora (language-reference)
**Why:** A whole-language skill is useful for its PROSE (guide/Book, std/library docs, idioms), not compiler internals.
**Corpora in brief doc_urls (post-merge):** {final_doc_urls_count}
  {for each entry: - {label}: {url}}
**Status:** {ATTACHED — {n_canonical} canonical corpora present | DEGRADED — code-only, no canonical corpora; attach the {language} guide + std docs before forging}
```

**Critical correction (red-team):** status is computed from the **final post-merge `doc_urls`** AN wrote to the brief context (the entries that will actually be fetched), **not** from the registry-seed count. So the report cannot claim "ATTACHED" for corpora that got dropped at brief-write. We do not render a per-entry `(source: registry|readme)` column — the brief schema discards provenance (`skf-write-skill-brief.py:438`), so that column would have no backing data. Provenance, if needed, lives only in the free-string `scope.notes`.

### EDIT 5 — mapping-table annotation (step-shape-detect.md §"Shape → Scope Type Mapping" line 56, and §"Heuristic Ladder" item 1 line 79)

Keep the mapping. Annotate the `language-reference` row and add a note block:

```markdown
| `language-reference` | `full-library` | Language tools/parsers are library-shaped from a skill perspective. **Corpora-dependent:** value comes from PROSE (guide/Book + std/library docs), not compiler internals — step-auto-scope.md §6a seeds companion corpora and §6/§7 emit an honest DEGRADED caveat when none are found (mirrors the §3b facet-coverage guard). |
```

Doc-only, no logic. Cross-references the §3b precedent already cited in this file.

## Part 2 — CORPUS SOURCING (C2): two-layer, AN-owned, fixed wiring

### LAYER 1 (belt, already shipped) — README detection + regex widening graft

BS §2 (`step-auto-brief.md:75`) already runs `skf-detect-docs.py --repo-url {source_repo}` for every source repo. Today it **misses `doc.rust-lang.org/book`** (verified: `_DOC_DOMAIN_RE` requires `docs.`). Graft Stance A's surgical recall fix:

### EDIT 7 — widen two regexes (`skf-detect-docs.py:89-97`)

```python
_DOC_DOMAIN_RE = re.compile(
    r"(?:docs?\.|\.readthedocs\.|wiki\.|documentation\.)",   # docs\. -> docs?\.  (catches doc.rust-lang.org)
    re.IGNORECASE,
)
_DOC_PATH_RE = re.compile(
    r"(?:/docs/|/documentation/|/api/|/reference/|/guide/|/wiki/|/book/|/std/|/library/|/tutorial/)",
    re.IGNORECASE,
)
```

This is per-language-agnostic, strictly improves *every* repo, and makes the belt redundant with the registry (true belt-and-suspenders) instead of decorative. **Per the empirical-validation memory: validate the widened regex against real rust/python/go/ts/ruby READMEs before push** (over-capture risk bounded by `_REJECT_URL_RE` + T3<T1 weighting + dedup, but must be measured).

### LAYER 2 (suspenders, NEW) — curated registry, brief-contract shape

**New helper `src/shared/scripts/skf-language-corpora.py`** — pure static lookup, no network, no `gh`. Generic key lookup over the data file. Output is the **brief `doc_urls` contract** `{url, label}` (NOT detect-docs shape — red-team fix), as a JSON array on stdout.

```
uv run src/shared/scripts/skf-language-corpora.py --language {language}
  exit 0 → JSON array [{"url":..., "label":...}, ...]   (registry hit)
  exit 1 → no entry for {language}                       (long-tail / unknown)
  exit 2 → bad args / unreadable data file
```

**New data file `src/shared/data/language-corpora.json`** — flat object keyed by lowercase language id. Internally each entry carries `content_type` for human readability, but the helper **emits only `{url, label}`** to match what the writer keeps. URLs verified live (HTTP 200) per the red-team; **TS reference URL changed** `utility-types.html` → the docs hub `/docs/` (red-team: utility-types is a narrow single page, misses the reference tier); **Python gains HOWTOs + PEP 8** and **Go gains the Tour** (red-team: deliver the promised "idioms"/onboarding):

```json
{
  "rust": [
    {"url": "https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/",        "label": "The Rust Programming Language (Book)"},
    {"url": "https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/",         "label": "Rust Standard Library"},
    {"url": "https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/",   "label": "The Rust Reference"}
  ],
  "python": [
    {"url": "https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/",    "label": "The Python Tutorial"},
    {"url": "https://docs.python.org/3/library/",     "label": "Python Standard Library"},
    {"url": "https://docs.python.org/3/reference/",   "label": "Python Language Reference"},
    {"url": "https://docs.python.org/3/howto/",       "label": "Python HOWTOs (idioms)"}
  ],
  "go": [
    {"url": "https://go.dev/tour/",                   "label": "A Tour of Go"},
    {"url": "https://go.dev/doc/effective_go",        "label": "Effective Go"},
    {"url": "https://go.dev/ref/spec",               "label": "The Go Language Specification"},
    {"url": "https://pkg.go.dev/std",                "label": "Go Standard Library"}
  ],
  "typescript": [
    {"url": "https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro.html", "label": "TypeScript Handbook"},
    {"url": "https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/",                    "label": "TypeScript Documentation (Reference)"}
  ],
